<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: runtime-adapters-tool-permissions-and-human-approval
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/runtime-adapters-tool-permissions-and-human-approval.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/runtime-adapters-tool-permissions-and-human-approval.qmd@c28d894f1
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Runtime Adapters, Tool Permissions, and Human Approval

An AI system becomes dangerous at the boundary where thought turns into
effect.

Before that boundary, a model may be wrong, persuasive, confused, or useful
without changing the world. After that boundary, it can write files, call
services, send messages, deploy code, spend money, use credentials, move
hardware, or alter an institutional record. The stack therefore needs a
specific object at this edge: a runtime adapter.

A runtime adapter is not just a tool wrapper. It is a capability lease for an
external effect. It decides which typed job may use which capability, against
which target, in which sandbox, under which approval, with which authority
handle, producing which receipt, rollback path, residual, and evidence record.

The design goal is simple: no external effect should be treated as just
another thought.

The previous two chapters bounded work and preserved its artifact trail. This
chapter handles the moment when a bounded job asks to touch external state.
That moment is where a record becomes authority, and where a missing approval,
stale lease, leaked handle, or vague rollback path can turn a good plan into a
bad effect.

The adapter chapter's specific addition is the external-effect lease. Many
parts of the stack produce records, but an adapter record has to decide whether
anything may actually change outside the system. Permission, approval, sandbox,
secret handle, pre-state, post-state, rollback, residual, and effect receipt
are the difference between thinking about an action and doing it.

## Problem

Plans become real-world effects only through tools, runtimes, deployment
adapters, and approval gates.

Labor OS turns accepted work into typed jobs. Artifact graphs preserve the
history of work products. But neither one should directly touch the world.
The runtime adapter is the narrow edge where a bounded job receives a bounded
authority lease.

This matters because tools are not neutral. A file edit may destroy evidence.
An API call may expose data. A deployment may change a public system. A
message may create obligations. A payment may move money. A credential may
open a channel the model should never see. A hardware action may create
physical risk. Those effects need a different control surface than ordinary
reasoning.

The adapter boundary is where the stack stops asking only "is this a good
idea?" and starts asking "is this action authorized, scoped, reversible,
auditable, and safe enough to attempt?"

## Why Tool Calling Is Not Enough

Tool calling without a permission and approval model turns reasoning errors
into side effects.

A tool call is often presented as a convenience: the model needs information,
so it calls search; it needs to edit code, so it calls the filesystem; it
needs to ship something, so it calls a deployment path. But a tool call is an
authority transfer. It gives cognition a route into external state.

External literature gives useful comparison points. ReAct connects reasoning
with actions and observations. Runtime-assurance systems such as Black-Box
Simplex show that advanced controllers can be wrapped by safety-oriented
switching logic. Runtime monitors such as Copilot show another tradition of
checking behavior while systems run. Proof-carrying code and Dafny point to
checkable obligations before code is trusted. Task-and-motion planning and
behavior trees expose action boundaries in robotics. These are orientation
sources, not local reproduction claims. This repository has not imported their
proofs, reproduced their benchmarks, or built an adapter assurance case from
them.

The ASI Stack's adapter claim is narrower: every external effect should pass
through a record that exposes capability, approval, receipt, rollback, and
residual state.

## Core Claim

Runtime adapters should enforce typed permissions, sandboxing, human approval,
and post-action evidence capture. In the live book, this claim remains a
design rationale at `argument` support.

Talos supplies controlled runtime and approval discipline. VIEA supplies the
intent-to-runtime feedback boundary. Ladon and the Manhattan Protocol supply
handle-mediated authority and secret isolation. GenesisCode supplies
capability-mediated effects and deterministic effect-log discipline. Software
Magic Grimoire supplies guarded workflow language. Field of God AI
Constitution supplies consent, reversibility, least sufficient power, risk
tiers, auditability, and no self-authorization. Theseus Operator OS supplies
operator-visible runtime control. MoECOT remains implementation-reference
context only until runtime artifacts, logs, replay records, or benchmark
records are imported and inspected.

The current repository validates a `runtime_adapter_invocation` fixture,
implements finite Lean predicates for adapter permission and high-impact
approval rejection, runs a synthetic runtime-adapter permission harness over
typed jobs, adapter invocations, and authority-use receipts, records a Runtime
adapter effect replay probe, and adds a Runtime adapter adversarial boundary
probe. The effect probe executes `valid_low_impact_local_write_effect_replay`
as one public-safe temp-file write, restores the pre-state digest as a
rollback-exact check, and blocks `invalid_missing_permission_no_mutation` plus
`invalid_expired_approval_no_mutation` before mutation. The adversarial
boundary probe checks two synthetic adapter boundary reviews and twelve
expected-invalid controls for parentage, authority, approval, sandbox, secret,
rollback, receipt, audit, support-state, and non-claim boundaries. These are
useful record-gate and toy-effect traces. They are not deployed adapter
execution, sandbox isolation, live approval-service behavior, secret-handle
safety, revocation propagation, rollback-service behavior, runtime security,
Talos runtime behavior, MoECOT runtime reproduction, or benchmark results.

## Mechanism

The adapter converts a typed job request into a scoped effect lease.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Job["Typed job"] --> Contract["Adapter contract"]
  Contract --> Capability["Capability check"]
  Capability --> Handle["Authority / secret handle"]
  Handle --> Approval{"Approval required?"}
  Approval -- "yes" --> Human["Scoped approval record"]
  Approval -- "no" --> Lease["Effect lease"]
  Human --> Lease
  Lease --> Invoke["Sandboxed invocation"]
  Invoke --> Receipt["Effect receipt"]
  Receipt --> Rollback["Rollback handle or residual"]
  Rollback --> Evidence["Artifact graph / evidence ledger"]
  Receipt --> Incident["Incident or audit path"]
```

The typed job names the intended work. The adapter contract names the target
type, requested capability, sandbox mode, input and output contract, approval
requirement, risk tier, and evidence obligations. Permission checks decide
whether the parent job is allowed to request that adapter capability. Authority
handles remain outside model-visible context unless a policy and approval
path authorizes their use.

If approval is required, the approval must be scoped. It should name the
target, risk tier, duration, allowed reuse, revocation path, approving role,
and audit references. A human "yes" to one deployment, message, payment,
credential use, or hardware action is not a permanent license for future
similar actions.

The human gate can also degrade. A reviewer may be tired, moving too quickly,
facing too many alerts, or leaning on the system's recommendation instead of
checking the evidence. That is human oversight degradation: approval fatigue,
rubber-stamping, alarm fatigue, and automation bias can make an approval record
look clean while the review underneath it has weakened. A serious adapter has
to route those states to delay, reviewer rotation, independent evidence checks,
or blocked dispatch.

Authority can also degrade across the network. If a worker can act while a
revocation, approval expiry, or lease update is partitioned elsewhere, the
adapter may see a stale grant that still looks usable. Partitioned authority is
the rule that protected effects need a fresh authority receipt, quarantine, or
no-mutation evidence before dispatch. A stale grant is not permission just
because it is available to the local process.

If the adapter runs, it returns an effect receipt. The receipt says what was
requested, what was approved, what was invoked, what changed, what verified,
what failed, what can be rolled back, what cannot be undone, and what evidence
or incident trail now exists.

## Effect Receipts

The effect receipt is the adapter's most important artifact.

A successful API response is not the same as a successful task. It may prove
only that the call returned. It does not prove that the call was authorized,
safe, useful, reversible, complete, or aligned with the parent plan.

The receipt should therefore separate:

- the job request;
- the adapter capability;
- the permission basis;
- the approval record;
- the authority handle;
- the sandbox mode;
- the pre-state reference;
- the post-state reference;
- the external side effects;
- the verification references;
- the rollback handle;
- the irreversible residuals;
- the incident references;
- the audit references;
- the support-state effect;
- the non-claims.

This is how the stack avoids receipt laundering. A tool call can succeed while
verification remains incomplete. A deployment can succeed while rollback is
missing. A message can be sent while residual obligations remain. The receipt
keeps those distinctions visible.

## A Message-Adapter Scenario

Imagine a typed job that asks the system to email a collaborator about a draft
chapter. Drafting the message is a low-impact artifact. Sending it is an
external effect. The adapter boundary keeps those states separate.

The job may allow the model to prepare a draft, attach cited public files, and
list questions for the user. It may not send the message until the adapter has
a current approval record naming the recipient, subject, allowed attachments,
expiry, and whether reuse is allowed. If the user approves only this message,
that approval cannot authorize a later follow-up, a different attachment, or a
message to a different recipient.

When the adapter sends the message, the effect receipt records the parent job,
approval, sandbox mode, authority handle, pre-send draft hash, post-send
delivery status, audit refs, rollback or correction path, and any irreversible
residual. If the mail service accepts the API call but the attachment was
wrong, the receipt is not a success story. It is an incident or residual with a
correction path.

This scenario does not claim a deployed mail adapter, live approval service,
sandbox isolation, secret-handle safety, rollback execution, or delivery
correctness. It shows the adapter's job: convert a thought into a scoped effect
only when permission, approval, receipt, and residual handling are explicit.

The same logic applies when authority state is stale. If the system cannot tell
whether a grant is current, sending the message is not safer merely because the
mail service is reachable; that partitioned authority boundary matters even
for ordinary communication tools. The adapter should ask for a fresh receipt or
preserve a no-mutation denial record.

## Interfaces

Runtime work reaches the world through the Runtime Adapter Invocation.

Execution owns adapters. Security mediates authority handles and secrets.
Human approval supplies scoped authorization. Evidence records effect
receipts, rollback handles, irreversible residuals, incident references, and
deployment outcomes. Procedural memory later examines repeated traces only
after the adapter record says what actually happened.

A useful adapter invocation record names:

- `invocation_id`
- `invocation_state`
- `job_id`
- `adapter_id`
- `target_type`
- `capability`
- `permission_required`
- `sandbox_mode`
- `impact_class`
- `risk_tier`
- `approval_required`
- `approval_record`
- `approval_scope`
- `approval_expiry`
- `authority_handle`
- `effect_lease`
- `effect_receipt`
- `pre_state_ref`
- `post_state_ref`
- `external_side_effects`
- `verification_refs`
- `rollback_handle`
- `irreversible_residuals`
- `incident_refs`
- `audit_refs`
- `residuals`
- `support_state_effect`
- `non_claims`

The adapter should be able to say no. Denial is not a failure of intelligence.
It is the point of the boundary when permission, approval, sandboxing,
rollback, or evidence is missing.

## Invariants

No adapter executes beyond granted scope.

High-impact actions require current approval.

Approval scope expires and does not authorize unrelated reuse.

Authority handles are not exposed to model-visible context merely because a
tool request exists.

Partitioned authority or a possible stale grant blocks protected dispatch
until the adapter has a fresh receipt or no-mutation evidence.

Rollback handles are captured when available, and irreversible residuals are
declared when rollback is unavailable.

Effect receipts separate request, approval, execution, verification, rollback,
residual, and support-state effect.

These invariants describe the desired boundary. The repository currently
checks finite record predicates and synthetic fixtures; it does not enforce
these invariants in a deployed adapter service.

## Failure Modes

Tool overreach occurs when an adapter performs beyond the granted permission
or target.

Approval bypass occurs when high-impact action proceeds without a current,
scoped approval record.

Approval laundering occurs when a narrow approval is reused as broad
authorization for similar but unapproved effects.

Human oversight degradation occurs when approval is present but unreliable:
the reviewer is overloaded, the rationale is template-only, alerts have become
noise, or the automation recommendation is accepted despite contradictory
evidence.

Partitioned authority occurs when approval, lease, or revocation state is no
longer fresh at the effect site. It is a stale-control failure: the local
process can still see a grant, but the architecture cannot honestly treat that
grant as current.

Receipt laundering occurs when a successful tool response is treated as
verified task success.

Secret-handle leakage occurs when authority meant to stay opaque becomes
model-visible context.

Rollback fiction occurs when the record claims reversibility without an actual
rollback path or without naming irreversible residuals.

Each failure should block dispatch, force escalation, create an incident
record, or leave a residual that future ledgers cannot ignore.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is a runtime-adapter invocation schema,
public fixtures, finite permission, approval, lease, rollback, dispatch,
effect-replay, adversarial-boundary, and revocation-route predicates, a
synthetic cross-record harness, one public-safe local effect replay, and one
deterministic adversarial boundary probe.

The repository currently has that record surface. The fixture validates
invocation state, impact class, risk tier, approval scope and expiry, effect
lease, pre/post state refs, external side effects, verification refs,
irreversible residuals, incident and audit refs, support-state effect, and
non-claims. `AsiStackProofs.RuntimeAdapters` proves finite predicates that a
valid invocation requires the parent job permission list to include the
adapter capability, that a high-impact unapproved invocation is rejected, that
revoked approvals, leases, and authority receipts do not dispatch without
no-mutation denial evidence, and that incomplete effect replays route to
no-mutation, rollback, receipt, or no-promotion-boundary evidence instead of
accepted replay.
`scripts/validate_runtime_adapter_permissions.py` checks synthetic typed-job,
adapter-invocation, authority-use-receipt, and authority-probe consistency for
permissions, approval gates, expiry markers, receipts, rollback handles,
irreversible residuals, authority receipt alignment, ambient-authority
confused-deputy rejection, and revoked-receipt blocking.

The partitioned-authority fixture adds the distributed stale-grant case. It
checks finite records for revocation-delay quarantine, fresh authority receipt
requirements, grant/effect race residual ownership, no-mutation evidence, and
support-state non-promotion. It is not a live partition test and does not prove
distributed consensus, availability, revocation propagation, or deployed
adapter enforcement.

The Runtime adapter effect replay probe adds the smallest executable slice:
`python3 scripts/run_runtime_adapter_effect_probe.py --write-result` creates a
generated temporary file outside the repository, dispatches a low-impact write
under the toy gate, records 178-byte pre-state and rollback digests, records a
289-byte post-state digest, and validates rollback-exact restoration through
`python3 scripts/validate_runtime_adapter_effect_probe.py`. The same probe
blocks a missing-permission request and an expired-approval request before
mutation. It records `repo_write=false`, `network_used=false`,
`support_state_effect=none`, and `chapter_core_support_effect=none`.

That slice is deliberately humble. It does not run a production sandbox,
mediate a real secret, conduct a live human approval workflow, validate a live
effect receipt, propagate revocation in a deployed service, execute rollback
in a target service, or prove tool safety.

The Runtime adapter adversarial boundary probe adds breadth to that humble
slice. `python3 scripts/validate_runtime_adapter_adversarial_boundary_probe.py`
records two valid synthetic adapter boundary reviews and twelve
expected-invalid controls. It rejects confused-deputy parent mismatch,
authority ceiling overrun, approval scope mismatch, expired approval, sandbox
escape path, secret materialized into model-visible context, missing rollback
handle, missing effect receipt, missing audit refs, support-state promotion,
and missing non-claim boundaries. It is still not deployed adapter execution;
it is a record-level adversarial gate. The new revocation-route proof closes
one more finite-record gap by showing where revoked approval, lease, and
authority-receipt records route, but it is still not deployed revocation
propagation.

The Human oversight degradation fixture adds one more record-level boundary:
approval can fail even when a human is present. It checks scoped low-fatigue
approval, overloaded-reviewer delay, automation-bias blocking, missing
qualification, rubber-stamping, alarm fatigue, support-state promotion from
approval shape, and missing non-claim boundaries. That fixture does not prove
reviewer correctness, approval-service quality, deployed human-factors
behavior, or tool safety. It prevents the chapter from treating "human in the
loop" as a magic phrase.

The next honest slice would run a deployed adapter fixture end to end in a
safe local environment: a low-impact local write with approval not required, a
high-impact dry-run deployment requiring approval, a stale-approval negative
case, a missing-permission negative case, and an irreversible-effect record
that is rejected unless residuals are explicit.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a capability firewall for AI effects.

In that endpoint, tools are not ambient functions. A typed job receives a
scoped, expiring effect lease only after the stack checks the target,
capability, sandbox, authority handle, approval requirement, risk tier,
pre-state, expected post-state, rollback path, evidence obligations, and
active governance constraints.

The adapter service can cover documents, code, APIs, services, databases,
deployments, messages, payments, credentials, hardware, fabrication, robotics,
and organizational workflows. Each target family can define its own effect
receipt, rollback semantics, incident path, and approval policy while still
sharing the same core adapter grammar.

The product-level endpoint is not faster tool calling. It is lease-bound
action: a system where every external effect carries a permission basis,
approval record, authority boundary, sandbox mode, receipt, verification
trail, rollback or residual record, and evidence-state effect.

That endpoint remains unimplemented here. The live claim stays at `argument`
until adapter harnesses, sandbox checks, approval workflows, secret-handle
tests, rollback records, effect-receipt validation, negative controls,
benchmarks, security reviews, and public-safe runtime traces justify a
stronger state.

## Evidence Boundary

The runtime-adapter layer argues for capability-mediated external effects. It does not
claim that the ASI Stack can safely use arbitrary tools.

Current evidence consists of source synthesis, passage-reviewed source
mappings, public schemas and fixtures, finite Lean predicates, a synthetic
runtime-adapter permission harness, one public-safe Runtime adapter effect
replay probe, one Runtime adapter adversarial boundary probe, and one Human
oversight degradation fixture. Those artifacts support the shape of the
control surface, one toy temp-file effect path, a broader synthetic denial
matrix, and a finite reviewer-degradation denial matrix. They do not prove
deployed adapter behavior, sandbox isolation, approval-service correctness,
reviewer correctness, deployed human-factors behavior, secret-handle safety,
live effect receipt validity, rollback-service behavior, incident response,
benchmark performance, security behavior, Talos runtime behavior, MoECOT
runtime reproduction, ReAct reproduction, Simplex-level assurance,
Copilot-style runtime monitoring, or proof-carrying-code enforcement.

The adapter exists to keep authority from leaking through convenience. The
chapter preserves the same discipline about its own evidence.

## Handoff

Runtime adapters produce the receipts that make repeated tool use learnable.
But repetition alone is not memory.

Procedural Memory and Cognitive Loop Closure takes adapter traces and asks
which patterns can be safely abstracted, parameterized, verified, registered,
monitored, and retired. The handoff is deliberately strict: a pattern becomes
memory only when its authority scope, failures, regressions, residuals, and
retirement triggers are visible.

This closes the action side of Part II. A job can be authorized, an artifact
can preserve what happened, and an adapter can constrain the effect. The
remaining question is whether repeated traces can become future capability
without bypassing the same boundaries that made the original work governable.
